content.full_text:
SUPERSEDED BY 1998
AMENDMENTS TO
REGULATION 18530

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 9, 1994

Hon. Larry Bowler
Assemblyman, 10th District
P.O. Box 942849
Sacramento, CA 94249-0001

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-94-276

Dear Assemblyman Bowler:

We write to confirm the telephone advice given by this agency
to Ms. Kelli Norton of the Assembly Republican Caucus regarding
the public expenditure prohibitions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").¹

Ms. Norton inquired whether or not Section 85300 of the Act
prohibited a member of the legislature from distributing, at
political events, informational brochures and copies of the public
service directory, both of which are published at state expense.
Ms. Norton indicated that these items are regularly published by
the legislature for distribution by its members to their
constituents.

Section 85300 prohibits the expenditure of public money for
any campaign for elective office. Under Section 85300, both the
expenditure by a public official, and the acceptance by a
candidate of a contribution of public funds, are prohibited.
Therefore, when public funds are expended to advocate or promote a
candidate's selection to public office, the Act is violated.
However, Section 85300 does not prohibit every government
expenditure which indirectly benefits a candidate for election.

Because the information and materials described by Ms. Norton
are published for distribution to the public, and their
publication is unconnected to any campaign for elective office,
the mere distribution during a political event sponsored by a
candidate for election does not violate Section 85300. This
advice is, of course, limited to the situation described herein
and would not extend to any situation where such materials were

_______________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. A-94-276
Page 2


sold to finance a campaign, or distributed solely to contributors
as a quid pro quo for a contribution.

We trust that this confirmation adequately addresses your
concerns.  Should you have any questions, please feel free to
contact the undersigned at (916) 322-5901.


Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By:  Daniel E. Muallem
     Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question: Whether or not Section 85300 of the Act prohibited a member of the legislature from distributing, at political events, informational brochures and copies of the public service directory, both of which are published at state expense.
sections.conclusion: Section 85300 does not prohibit every government expenditure which indirectly benefits a candidate for election. Because the information and materials described are published for distribution to the public, and their publication is unconnected to any campaign for elective office, the mere distribution during a political event sponsored by a candidate for election does not violate Section 85300. This advice is limited to the situation described and would not extend to any situation where such materials were sold to finance a campaign, or distributed solely to contributors as a quid pro quo for a contribution.
sections.question_synthetic: Does Section 85300 of the Political Reform Act prohibit legislators from distributing state-funded informational brochures and public service directories at political campaign events?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Distribution of state-published informational materials at political events does not violate Section 85300 because the materials are published for general public distribution and are unconnected to any campaign. However, this advice does not permit selling such materials to finance a campaign or distributing them solely to contributors as quid pro quo.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document marked as 'SUPERSEDED BY 1998 AMENDMENTS TO REGULATION 18530'. Clear question and conclusion sections present. Telephone advice was confirmed in writing.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether or not Section 85300 of the Act prohibited a member of the legislature from distributing, at political events, informational brochures and copies of the public service directory, both of which are published at state expense.

CONCLUSION: Section 85300 does not prohibit every government expenditure which indirectly benefits a candidate for election. Because the information and materials described are published for distribution to the public, and their publication is unconnected to any campaign for elective office, the mere distribution during a political event sponsored by a candidate for election does not violate Section 85300. This advice is limited to the situation described and would not extend to any situation where such materials were sold to finance a campaign, or distributed solely to contributors as a quid pro quo for a contribution.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advised Assemblyman Larry Bowler that legislators may distribute state-funded informational brochures and public service directories at political events without violating Section 85300's public expenditure prohibitions, provided the distribution is incidental and not designed to finance campaigns or reward contributors.
